hook.text: Every element absorbs specific wavelengths of light. By splitting starlight, we can identify what stars are made of.

buildup.text: Joseph Fraunhofer noticed dark lines in the Sun's spectrum in 1814. Each line corresponds to a chemical element.

discovery.text: Kirchhoff and Bunsen matched the lines to lab spectra in the 1860s, launching the field of astrophysics.

twist.text: Helium was discovered on the Sun before it was found on Earth. Its name comes from Helios—the Greek sun god.

climax.text: Today, spectroscopy reveals a star's composition, temperature, speed, and even whether it has planets.

punchline.text: Split the light, read the recipe. Every star reveals its secrets.

quiz.question: What can spectroscopy reveal about a star?
options: Its chemical composition, temperature, speed, and potential planets
Only whether it is a main sequence or giant star
